Integrity Trends Analysis Tool (ITAT)
Completed by Statistics Canada
Introduction and Response by IRCC

Introduction to Integrity Trends Analysis Tool by IRCC
Background
Automated tools are part of Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship Canada (IRCC)’s commitment to
using technology responsibly to build a stronger immigration system for all of our clients. In January
2023, IRCC launched a technical system, the Integrity Trends Analysis Tool (ITAT), previously known
internally as “Watchtower” or “Lighthouse.” ITAT is a data mining tool that leverages advanced analytics
techniques to support efficient and effective processing by surfacing risk patterns for investigation by
Risk Assessment Units1. ITAT enhances the effectiveness and consistency of IRCC’s fraud detection and
verification activities.
As required by the Treasury Board Secretariat’s (TBS) Directive on Automated Decision-Making, a peer
review of this tool was conducted by the Data Science Team at Statistics Canada.
The goal of the peer review was to provide an informed assessment of whether the technology is being
used responsibly by validating IRCC’s Algorithmic Impact Assessment, the quality of the system, the
appropriateness of the quality assurance and risk mitigation measures, as well as to identify the residual
risk of operating the system.
IRCC used the findings from this peer review to improve the tool, the tool’s governance, and the tool’s
Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) prior to ITAT’s launch in January 2023.
ITAT: Purpose, Functionality, Launch
ITAT analyzes large volumes of data and extracts various risk and fraud patterns within applications
containing adverse characteristics, such as inadmissibility findings (e.g. criminality and
misrepresentation) and other administrative or enforcement actions related to the Immigration and
Refugee Protection Act (IRPA) and Regulations (IRPR). Risk Assessment Units are aware that an
application is flagged by the tool and decide when to initiate verification activities. However, front line
decision-making officers are not informed of ITAT inputs related to the applications that they are
processing.
Front line decision-making officers review applications and make decisions consistent with procedural
fairness and per the requirements of the IRPA/IRPR. If verification activities are performed, decision-
making officers only see verification results and may consider the results when making decisions.
Examples of verification activities include authenticating proof of funds from a bank or verifying a letter
of acceptance from a Designated Learning Institution (DLI). Consequently, separate and apart from ITAT,
1 Risk Assessment Unit are groups of specialized officers who focus on evaluating and managing risks related to
immigration and refugee applications. They identify fraud trends and verify the authenticity of documents,
ensuring that the work of front-line decision-makers is grounded in a comprehensvie understanding of potential
risks.
3
a processing officer may request a verification activity, or it may be initiated independently by a Risk
Assessment Unit if a fraud trend is identified.
The Risk Assessment Units may use various techniques including telephone calls, site visits, or in-person
interviews as a means to validate the risks. The results of the verification activities are shared with
decision-making officers whether or not adverse information was found. ITAT is designed to assist Risk
Assessment Units in identifying applications for verification activity by automating the detection of risk
patterns associated with adverse outcomes from historical data and flagging new applications that
match these patterns.
ITAT’s risk patterns are not visible to decision-making officers. Rather, decision-making officers only
receive the results of the verification activities performed by Risk Assessment Units, which may have
been initiated with or without the tool. As such, ITAT does not automate recommendations or decisions
on applications.
In 2023, ITAT for the temporary resident programs was implemented in a phased approach. It was
initially used on study permit applications and subsequently expanded to temporary resident visa
applications and temporary foreign worker applications. The subsequent phase will involve the
integration of eTA (electronic travel authorization). Continuous evaluation will be undertaken to ensure
ongoing assurance of the tool’s performance and responsible use. This approach seems systematic and
thorough, ensuring the tool’s effectiveness while also maintaining accountability.
Preface: Insights from the ITAT Peer Review
IRCC used the findings from this peer review, completed in 2021, to improve the tool, the tool’s
governance, and the tool’s Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) prior to ITAT’s launch in January 2023.
As the basis for the peer review, Statistics Canada’s Data Science Division reviewed many aspects of the
tool and its governance, including ITAT’s Algorithmic Impact Assessment and the proposed quality
assurance plan for monitoring the tool post-deployment. Statistics Canada’s Data Science Division also
evaluated documents outlining the purpose of ITAT, the business requirements it is meant to satisfy,
and the algorithms governing its operation. These documents provided a detailed understanding of the
data ITAT takes as input, the logical rules the tool applies to this data, and the resulting outputs.
4
Peer Review Report by Statistics Canada
Introduction
This document provides a peer review of the Watchtower Risk Identification Prototype. The core
purpose is to inform the team responsible for this prototype of potential ethical or quality concerns. The
review has been done by relying on Statistics Canada (2021) Responsible Machine Learning Framework.
Bronson and Millar (2020) propose a high-level process for peer review under Canada’s Directive on
Automated Decision-Making which was also consulted. Moreover, we have examined the Algorithmic
Impact Assessment form as it has been filled by the team responsible for the Watchtower project.
Key Recommendations:
1. We recommend describing, with concrete examples, the kind of decisions/actions that the IRCC
officials will be able to make/take with this tool in order for the readers to better understand the
benefits and risks associated with this project. What will the agents do with the information? What
can happen to the applicants? Are the applicants in Canada or a different country? It would be good
to define the expression ‘adverse information’ more rigorously. Is it an euphemism for ‘crime’ or
‘offense’?
2. The algorithm associates “data patterns to adverse information” p.5. We recommend using more
standard terminology such as “independent variables to dependent variables” or “features to
labels”. This will improve the interpretability of the documentation of the project.
3. Based on the information in the document, this looks like a predictive machine learning (ML) project
where one models the relationships between the variables in the dataset. How can this tool provide
“fact-based information that end users (IRCC officers) may find relevant for their adjudication
process” p.5 if the officers are not going to classify/make predictions on individuals? Expressions
such as “identify only those patterns suggestive of organized risk” p.8 imply predictive labeling.
Therefore, we recommend removing expressions such as “the tool is not predictive”p.5.
4. If this is a predictive modelling project, then a train/validation/test methodology must be applied. If
there is no need for validation, then it should be explained. The metrics that will be used should be
described and justified. How do you measure the “utility and timeliness” p.14?
5. We recommend a more thorough description of the quality of the data set that will be used by the
algorithm. Does the data fail to cover a subpopulation that could be at risk? Are there any missing
data? If so, has there been any kind of imputation (without leakage)? Do you use all of the available
data to run the algorithm or only the observations that have been previously flagged by officers?
6. We recommend answering the following two questions. Will the information used reflect and
exacerbate stereotypes? We understand that the purpose is not to identify a “broad subpopulation
that tends to have higher adverse rates” but will someone monitor this over time?
5
7. We recommend describing how the quality of the algorithm will be maintained over time. How will
you measure the “utility and timeliness” p.14 over time? Will an individual be investigated over and
over
8. It would be good to describe the alternative methods that have been considered, like decision trees,
and to explain why they have not been chosen.
9. We recommend that this report should be approved by the Deputy Minister in charge of the
program that will use the automated decision system.
10. We recommend the publication of this report and the completed Algorithmic Impact Assessment on
the Open Government Portal prior to deploying the system.
Minor Recommendations:
1. We recommend explaining how this tool will ultimately benefit Canadians in terms that they will
understand. This can help motivate the need for such measures.
2. We recommend indicating that some research has been done (bibliography) on the ethical use of
machine learning for decision-making with crime data.
6

b) Preference for qualitative user testing over traditional validation
methods: This approach involves close collaboration with end users to
manually assess historical patterns identified by the system. The system
generates interpretable historical pattern information, enabling risk
assessment officers to make assessments solely on the face of the
information presented. This contrasts against typical ML systems that
would use less interpretable output (binary recommendations, scores,
etc.) where there is a much higher reliance on showing test-sample
performance as a means to validate the system. This qualitative user-
testing approach is in many ways superior to using quantitative test set
results, particularly given the lack of ground truth labels. It also
reinforces the point that the system should merely identify factual
information that can stand on its own merits, rather than expecting
reliance on past out-of-sample test performance to validate the usage. It
emphasizes presenting relevant information rather than predicting
outcomes, providing a more relevant performance assessment.

More importantly, the impact of a false positive on the system is minimal
and possibly even favorable for the applicant. In the above example, a false
positive would involve the system identifying a pattern that, upon human
review, is deemed sufficient to merit conducting a verification with the
school and with that verification confirming the applicant is indeed
registered. In this scenario, the impact is possibly a slight delay in
processing but increasing the applicant’s likelihood of approval. This careful
design aims to mitigate any negative consequences from false positives.
7. We recommend describing how the 7. The high-level plan is to regularly shift the time window in which patterns

8. It would be good to describe the 8. Early testing considered several alternative methods, including C5 decision
alternative methods that have been trees. However, our current approach, association rule mining, is preferred
considered, like decision trees, and to for two primary reasons related to how patterns are identified and the need
explain why they have not been chosen. for transparency.
The first reason pertains to how decision trees and association rules process
data. Decision trees partition the dataset by recursively splitting it into
mutually exclusive nodes, aiming to maximize predictive accuracy or
minimize classification error. The structure limits their ability to identify
smaller, highly associated clusters of applications. In contrast, association
rule mining focuses on detecting strong relationships within localized
subsets of data, without the restriction of mutual exclusivity. This makes it
particularly effective at uncovering small but meaningful patterns that may
not exhibit broad, dataset-wide connections.
The second reason is the need for transparency and interpretability.
Concerns were raised about the lack of transparency in decision trees and
other algorithmic options, making it difficult to clearly understand or explain
the patterns they produce. Since full transparency is essential for this
system, association rule mining is the preferred approach.
9
Other methods were also considered but were found to be less effective at
capturing the complex, interrelated patterns required for the analysis.
9. We recommend that this report 9. In accordance with the TBS Directive on Automated Decision-Making the
should be approved by the Deputy Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) is responsible for the Algorithmic Impact
Minister in charge of the program that will Assessment and applicable requirements (i.e., the peer review.) To transition
use the automated decision system. from pilot to permanent tool, the Senior ADM of Service Delivery and SADM
of Strategic and Digital Policy approved a memorandum in September 2022.
This memo approved publication of the AIA to the Open Government Portal
and reviewed all TBS and IRCC governance requirements. Approval to launch
was given by the Director General Steering Committee. This peer review was
circulated and responded to throughout the governance process but Deputy
Minister approval as a distinct document was not required by TBS. That
being said, in accordance with this recommendation, IRCC will share the peer
review report with our deputy minister for approval. In January 2023 the
tool was phased into the temporary resident programs: students, followed
by visitors, and concluding with workers. Continuous evaluation and
reporting to senior management at IRCC will be undertaken to ensure
ongoing assurance of the tool’s performance and responsible utilization.
